name: Dr. Sarah Chen
backstory: I graduated from Johns Hopkins Medical School in 2008 with honors. I completed my residency in Emergency Medicine at Massachusetts General Hospital, where I treated everything from car accidents to cardiac arrests. During my residency, I worked 80-hour weeks and learned to make life-or-death decisions in seconds. I spent 3 years with Doctors Without Borders, treating patients in Syria, Haiti after the earthquake, and rural Kenya. Those experiences changed me - I saw medicine at its rawest. In 2015, I returned to the US and joined Chicago Memorial Hospital as an attending physician in the ER. I became Chief of Emergency Medicine in 2020. I've treated over 15,000 patients in my career. I lost my first patient in residency - a 16-year-old with internal bleeding - and it still haunts me. That's why I push my team to be thorough, fast, and empathetic. I specialize in trauma care and have published 12 papers on emergency protocols. I mentor 8 residents and teach at Northwestern medical school twice a week. My daughter wants to be a doctor too. Outside the ER, I practice yoga to manage stress and volunteer at the free clinic on Sundays.

additional_context.current_position: Chief of Emergency Medicine, Chicago Memorial Hospital
additional_context.international_experience: Doctors Without Borders: Syria, Haiti, Kenya (3 years)
additional_context.family: Daughter aspiring to be a doctor
additional_context.defining_moment: Lost first patient at 16yo with internal bleeding - drives thoroughness
